So you heard me talk about, you know, when we got into the nitty gritty details of the writing of copy of word choice of sentence structure of that sort of stuff, that one of the very first things that I talked about was the, which you've heard me say many times before, that good copy is verb driven, right? It's powered by verbs that good copy isn't filled with tons of adjectives and adverbs and how it's really, it's really driven by verbs. That's not to say you heard me talk about, right? We still use adjectives, we still use adverbs, it's just that the kind of the foundation of the copy is all about, is all about movement, is all about action, is all about things taking place, is kind of this feel of things happening. And that feel, right, you know, take a step back for one second, you know, even going back to what Chris, what you asked last week or what you talked about last week, you know, about the feel of the promotion for the copy workshop and how it felt exciting and how it felt dramatic and it felt big and it felt like it just kind of built on itself and kind of, you know, the momentum increase, right? There is a feel to copy, obviously, and we control that feel by certainly what we say and how we say it and where we say it. And a big part of that, that a lot of kind of, you know, that a lot of marketers, a lot of, even just a lot of communicators don't understand, is that part of that feel, that energy comes from their being movement and action in what's being communicated. And it's a weird thing to kind of articulate and to describe because it's, it's, you know, it is, it's a little bit hard to pinpoint, but when you don't have verb driven copy or when you, when, when your stuff is more based on adjectives, you know, descriptors, when it's adjectives and nouns and just adjectives and nouns, you know, nouns or person, place or thing, adjective is describing the, that person, place or thing, it has this feel of static, right? It has this feel of there's nothing happening while we're talking about a lot and we're describing a lot and we're sharing a lot. There's nothing really happening. It has this feel of stagnant because it is because there is no action, no movement being conveyed, that movement, that action, that things happening, things changing, things evolving, things transforming, things coming about that weren't there before, things disappearing that, that are there that are painful that we no longer want. That only comes about through verbs. Verbs are the action that is, that's, that's happening. And so, so, right, when, when I talk about, you know, verb driven, that's really what I'm talking about. I'm talking about conveying action, conveying change, conveying transformation, conveying this evolution, conveying going from here to here. And you heard me talk about on the workshop this idea of good verbs and weak verbs or powerful verbs, strong verbs and weak verbs, right? And number one, we, you know, we talked about, you know, that a good verb is a verb that appeals to one or more of the sentences. And so, right? Like when, you know, there are, there are some words that, that there are some verbs, there are some words in general that don't appeal to any one sense, meaning you can't see it, you can't really feel it, you know, there, it again, it is, there's no dimension to it. So when we've talked about before, you know, dimensionalized benefits, putting a picture to it, taking a functional benefit, what could be this vague general kind of somewhat benefit and, and adding dimension to it, making it something that they can see that they can feel that they can touch that they can hear that they can taste. Well, that also carries over to, to verbs, right, to word choice. There are some verbs that don't appeal to any one of the senses where you can't really see anything like he was behind the counter. You know, the idea of was, was being the, the, the, the, the verb he was, you know, behind the counter. Well, that's a verb and you could make the case that that is verb driven. There's right, like you're, you're putting somebody, you can't see that that's a weak verb. That's a dead verb, right, because you can't picture it in your head. And if we swapped out, you know, was behind the counter, right, he lurked behind the counter, right, is now you can at least conjure an, an, an, an image, um, you know, he hid behind the counter. Now you have a different image, right, still verb driven, but you've got action, you've got, now you can picture it. And so the difference between he was behind the counter and he lurked behind the counter, or he hid behind the counter, or he cowered behind the counter, though the, the, the, the difference in that one word conveys a wildly different picture. And so, right, that should make sense to you guys from the, the workshop, right. And a lot of that, a lot of this, putting this into practice and into play or putting it into play takes practice. It takes, right, when you're, when you're putting action into your copy, it takes, right, asking yourself, am I using the best verb, right? Am I using the best verb? In a moment, I'm going to tell you, I'm going to give you a kind of a trick or a, a, an approach that you can use to start to identify the right, at least the right group of, of verbs to choose from. But what was I going to say? This takes practice. It takes first, first, first and foremost, it takes, you know, intentionality or being intentional. It takes actively looking at your copy and looking through this lens of like, is there stuff happening? Is there movement? Is there action? Is, are things coming about? Are things disappearing? Are, are, are things now happening? Right? Is there movement? And so, what I want to do is I want to, I want to bring this up. I'm going to share my screen because I want to give you a very simple thing here. A very simple exercise to use, like a very simple kind of criteria, if you will, to start to bring this home. And so, so when we've got something like, you know, get more hair on your head, get being the, you know, the verb. And so, we could certainly say that that's, you know, a little bit of a flat, you know, there's like so many other words that we could use to replace, get, to create a better dimensionalized image. But here's the question. How do you know what, what is the right verb to use? So when we go back to that example of this, let's go back to the example of Bob was, whoops, got Bob was behind the counter. And so, the way that was being this dead, this dead verb, right, is the way that I begin to think about the right verb is, what is the image that I'm trying to convey? Right? Like, what is the image that I'm trying to convey? And oftentimes, when it comes to, let's say, you know, if we're talking about, let's say, a benefit, right? Typically, these, you know, typically, when we're using verb driven stuff, we're talking about benefits, you'll get this, you'll experience this, you'll see this, you'll watch this, you'll enjoy this, all that kind of stuff. Well, what I ask myself is, what's the image that I'm trying to convey? Am I trying to convey something related to speed? Like, am I trying to say that this is fast? Am I trying to say that this is easy? Like, or convey that this is easy? Am I trying to convey that this is simple? Am I trying to convey that this is done for you? Right? That this is almost a passive experience? Like, my thinking is, what is it that I am trying to convey? Because what it is that I'm trying to convey is going to help me narrow down the types of verbs or the different verbs that I could use that would convey fast. Right? Because, or let's say, or a lot, meaning if I'm trying to convey volume, right? So, I start to think about, what am I trying to convey? If we were saying, right, like, Bob was behind the counter, well, are we, are we trying to convey that he was afraid? Right? Like, I might say, you know, Bob shivered behind the counter, you know, or I might say, you know, he crouched behind the counter, I might say, right, he hid behind the counter, he ducked behind the counter, I might say, he cowered behind the counter. Right? Those are verbs that are appropriate for, you know, for conveying that he was scared, that, right, that he was hiding, he was trying to stay out of the phrase, so to speak. But right, if I wanted to convey that he was, you know, whatever, he was getting ready for action, right, maybe he was arming himself behind the counter, could be that he was readying himself behind the counter. Right? And so you see how those different verbs are conveying a completely different image. And the same thing with, you know, with things like with fast, with easy, with simple, with done for you, like passive, you're the recipient, or a lot, right? Like, so, you know, so my point, the takeaway, and we could certainly unpack this a little bit more if you guys want to, but there are different words that are connected, that that different verbs that would convey fast, like that this is happening fast, right? Like, you know, if we said, I don't know, you know, if we said something like, you know, like, look, Bob, you know, came on the scene, right? And so if we wanted to convey meaning, you know, he came on the scene, right? So kind of, you know, what does that mean? How did he, how did he, how did he come onto the scene? Like, what, right? How, how, what could we say? What could we say that would convey speed? What other verb? But like, so we're describing now that Bob, he entered your, right? Or Bob entered the scene, right? What could we say? Rushed it. Yeah, you could say Bob rushed right, rushed into the scene. That certainly conveys speed, right? But if we said, you know, Bob strolled into the scene, still verb driven, that's strolled, not stalled, strolled, strolled onto the scene, totally different picture, right? And, and, and so the, you know, what else, what else could we say besides rushed? Flu? Yeah, flew into the scene. What else? He appeared, like, appeared gives the feeling of quickly got there. Maybe I see that's a, that's an example. That's a, that's a good, uh, that's a good, it's a verb that you can use, right? Like it's, you know, it's a, it's a verb that you could use. That's an example of, right? And this is a great point and a great example. I would say to myself, does that really convey the type of speed that I want? Like, is that going, is that word, Bob appeared on the scene? Is that, I say to myself, is that a verb that is going to convey the type of speed that I want to, I want to convey, right? Like, or is there a stronger word? So if I really want to convey speed, like in this, in this image, and I'm saying, well, I could say Bob appeared on the scene or Bob rushed onto the scene. Well, I would probably be more apt to choose rushed onto the scene. I might look for another, another word. He, you know, he leaped onto the scene, you know, he flew onto the scene or went into the scene, you know, some ran onto the scene. Maybe he ran under the scene, right? Like another words. My point is that, um, number one, I'm asking myself first and foremost in terms of the, the, the verb, like, what am I trying to convey? Right? Okay. I'm trying to convey that. He, you know, he, he came onto the scene. Okay. But I'm also trying to convey speed. And so came onto the scene, doesn't convey speed. And so what is the word that I can use, which would convey speed better than came on the scene? We're still illustrating and demonstrating the same, the same image. It's still the same movement, but I want to make sure that it's clear that it is speed that is right that, that we're conveying. Just like now let's flip it and say, we want to, we want to convey, um, that, you know, that Bob was, you know, slow, into the scene. Right. And so I certainly could say, which is very basic, which is, you know, telling Bob was slow onto the, onto the scene. But if you look at this, I want you to notice something here. See what this is, is right? This is a weak verb with a descriptor. Right. See, this is a weak verb. Bob was slow onto the scene. We would be much better saying Bob strolled onto the scene. Right. Rather than was slow, right? Um, you know, like, uh, and so this is a much better sentence. You've got the, the subject, you've got the action over here, right. And the setting of the action. And so what else do you think we could say besides Bob strolled onto the scene? Crawled. Yeah. I mean, right. You, you legitimately could say Bob crawled, uh, you know, onto the scene. Right. Little bit of a different picture, but you're absolutely right. You could say that. What do you think, Chris? Lumbered onto the screen, onto the screen. Maybe. Right. Like, under the scene, right. Could, you know, um, I sometimes ask myself, right, with, with certain, with certain words, certain verbs, like, am I communicating extra stuff that I don't want to, you know, communicate? So, right. For me, lumbered, is that like, am I, is he heavy? Is he, does he have an injury? Like, is, did he stumble? Like, is that what I'm also trying to incorporate in there or, or no, do I just want to say like he, he, he strolled, or do I want to say, you know, he eased into the scene, you know, or something like that. Or, um, or I might say, you know what, I want to come up with a, um, a word that I, what, what's a verb that, uh, that demonstrates, um, hesitancy, right? So rather than say he hesitantly entered the scene, right, I'd probably go to Google or go to powerthysaurus.com and look up for like, hesitantly walked or hesitantly entered or what, what hesitantly like, and, and see if I could come up with a singular verb that demonstrates, you know, um, that, um, how he entered the, the, the scene. But the, the point, the ultimate takeaway is that you need to really be fully aware of what it is that you are trying to convey the image and the feeling. So it's not just verb driven, but it is when, when we talk about, you know, like, um, when we talk about like, let's say this, you know, um, get more hair, right? Like, get more hair. Or let's say, you, maybe you could say grow more hair. Certainly. I don't know if that's, you know, if that necessarily conveys, uh, probably a better way to talk about it is, um, if we want it to say, um, you know, speed, like, let's say this, get more, um, get more leads or, uh, so what could we say instead of get? Acquire. Okay. You could say acquire. I don't know if I would say that that changes, um, you know, uh, uh, um, changes necessarily the vibe that we're getting. What if we just said, what's the difference between attract and get? Well, one, you're doing the work. The other, you're not. You could make, right? Like attract more, uh, almost, right? Even though it's a little subtle thing, right? It has the vibe or a little bit more of a feeling that people are coming to you, right? Like there's something about you that's making people come to you and you see how just that, that the difference, right? So it's, it's not just, you know, you, you guys have heard me say before, like also, right? And there's, that's why, you know, there's a lot of these little things that are going on, right? Like I like to look for, you know, different, I like to use different words. I don't like to use the same words that, um, all, you know, like I try to mix it up a little bit. And so everybody in their brother is talking about, you know, attract more leads. What I'll do is I will, I'll do something like this. I'll go over to powerthesaurus.com and I'll put in attract and let's see, you know, sometimes you got to dig in here. You know, draw more, not really the best. Pull, uh, invite, you could go ahead. The one that's coming up for me is like flood, like flood your business with more leads, but then that's adding more words to the sentence. So, but so, yeah, but so, okay. So let's talk about that for one second as well. So in terms of priority, right? So I know, right, we talk about, you know, Piffy and tight, right? Like I want Piffy, I want tight copy. I want sharp. I want punchy, man. Like I want to hit it. I don't want to be, you know, verbose, but what takes priority is I want to make sure that I paint the image that I want to paint. So, right? Like, um, you know, clarity, right? Yeah. Like I want, I want two things, right? I want clarity, but I also want to make sure that I'm clear on the image that I'm trying to convey, right? And so like, I want to be, I want to first and foremost, it almost starts with this. It starts with what's the image that I'm trying to convey? What's the feeling that I'm trying to get? So if I'm trying to, if I'm trying to convey, right, like that you, you know, let's say, let's say, you know, get more leads in your business, right? I might, I might say, right? Flood your business with more leads. Now, like, look, this one might have another word, more words might be a little bit, but I want to start with what's the image that I am trying to convey. So get more leads in your business compared to flood more, flood your business with leads, flood your business with leads, conveys volume, right? Like it conveys a mass of people, whereas get more leads in your business doesn't. And so if I wanted to convey this volume of leads, I'm going to start with flood your business with more leads, then I'm going to ask myself, is flood the right word? Is flood the word that conveys the image the way that I want? Is it like number one? Is it does it convey the image that I want? You know, I might, you know, like, is that the word that I want to use? Well, okay, so yeah, that does convey the image. Let's say that I want to convey in this instance, because I want to convey volume. But all right, flood your business. That's a little bit, let's say I realize it's played out, like a lot of people say that, right? So I would then say, well, what other word could I use instead of flood that would still convey volume, but that would change it up a little bit would feel a little bit a little bit different, right? Like we could say, pack your business with more more leads, you know, you could say this isn't the right word, obviously, but you could say stuff your business with more leads, right? Not not the right image that we're trying to necessarily convey. But you see how I start with the image, the picture that I'm trying to paint, the feeling that I want them to get what I want them to understand. And, and then I will look for the right word or the better word. And if that means I got to get a little bit more verbose, I'm okay with it as long as I'm not making it unclear. So we're balancing this, we're balancing what's the picture that we want to create? Like what are we trying to say? Right? We're not because we have the ability and that's the beauty with verb driven copy. We have the ability to to communicate more than just something is happening. We have the ability to communicate how it's happening, the speed at which it's happening, the ease at which it's happening. And so that's the that's the nuance, right? That it's not just I want verbs and I want to focus on verbs and action, right? Because you can end up with is and was type of verbs like these dead verbs. You could also end up with the wrong verb where like, you know, you're, you're, you're, you know, like if we said, you know, like, again, if we were trying to convey speed, and we said something like, you know, bobby's into the scene, well, that's not, that's the wrong image that we're trying to create. And so even though he's like, even though there's motion, there's action happening, even though it's verb driven, it's not the right image that we want to create. And so you've got it. So quick question on that. So like when so when you're thinking of the image that you want to create, and you're dealing with something that at least in the online world is abstract, like leads or customers, like what are leads or customers look like in the online world? Are they stats on your dashboard? Are they, you know, number of contacts in your database? Like what is that? How do you paint that picture of this thing that is, I could do it offline in a physical store, right? But like online, what is that picture of a flood of leads or like, what does that look like? Yeah, I think what, and that's a great question. And I'm not sure if I have a great answer for that. Because you're right, some things, you know, you know, some things are, you know, are, are more abstract than other things. Where my mind goes is, is when I think about, let's just say, getting more leads, for example, and I am, we know, all right, like our audience, let's say our audience wants more leads, let's just say that. I, where my mind goes is, what is the indicator of that, that would get them most excited? Like, in other words, is it, let's say, you know, seeing your, you know, seeing the total number of contact records in your, in your CRM, you know, growing, knowing that, you know, every other day that you're sending out an email, you're sending it out to hundreds more people or, you know, or something like that. Or is it, like, or are we talking to people that, let's say, for them, it is looking at a chart on a dashboard, right? Which for me, like, that's not really, you know, what gets people excited about getting more leads is this growing audience of people that they can expose their message to, right? So, my mind goes to, like, having more people exposed to your, to your message, right? Because in, in that, there's this implication that the more people that are exposed to your message, all else being equal, the more sales you're going to get, the more money you're going to, you're going to get, right? People associate, if I could get my message out in front of a million people, right, I'm going to make a lot more money than if I only got it out in front of a thousand people. And so, while I don't know if there's like, I don't have a great answer for you, Teddy, my, I just try to think about, you know, what is an image that, that of this thing that the audience would get most excited about? Like, if I talk about analytics, if I talk about, like, you know, seeing the counter on your analytics go up every day, well, to me, that's not, that's not nearly as exciting. You know, your, your analytics going up every day as it counts more leads. Like that's, while, you know, while we're talking about getting more leads, that's not, it's the idea of knowing that more and more people every day are getting your marketing, right? Knowing that more and more people are being exposed to your offer every day, knowing that you're right, you're increasing the, the odds of making more and more sales every day. That's where I go. Does that make sense? Yeah, no, that makes perfect sense. And there's, and makes more sense to say that, then the generic, get more leads, get more customers, get more sales. Yeah. Like, I think like, look, man, you know, it goes back to, and look, I, I still have to practice this and be intentional about this. To this day, the, you know, the idea of like, look, you know, even, you know, get, get more sales every day or, or even this idea of get consistent sales, right? That is a very, that's a very superficial level benefit. While it's a benefit, right? Like you'll get sales every day, right? Like that's, you know, that is a very vague, general, common benefit that shared. What is more exciting is when we dimensionalize it, right? And that's like what made Clayton so freaking good was that he was able to turn that functional benefit, that superficial benefit into a dimensionalized picture that was universal enough to apply to the overwhelming majority, but was yet was still dimensionalized. And so like, so like compound, compound, you're checking account balance daily instead of, Yeah, it's, you know, like, look, it's even like, let me show you this year, let me, because I showed, I don't know if you were on for this, but I'll just show this real quick. Where was it here? One second. Where the heck was it on this? I see. Loaded metaphor active pronoun placement. One second. Yeah. So when I, when I talked about like, you know, and this is where, you know, like, and I know, I think Teddy, you've asked in some shape or form kind of or Damien is certainly, we've talked about this, right? Like, so you paint this, you know, this dimensionalized picture. So if we said, right, like, you get sales every day, well, dimensionalizing at that is kind of painting a picture of what the experience could be like, right? And it's like, you heard me say on the workshop, it's the picture, it's the being able to see it in your mind's eye that triggers the emotion. Those vague, very general, like, you know, and certainly the more vague and the more general a benefit is, the less emotionally impactful it is, because I get it logically, like, all right, you'll get sales consistently. Like, I get that logically, like that's a good thing, right? And I want that, but it doesn't trigger this, this picture in my head, right? And so it doesn't have this deep emotional response. Now, like, like, look, what some people do naturally, and, you know, some people, they'll like consistent sales, what happens is that in their mind, they'll start to, oh, yeah, man, that would be sick if every day I was, but what they're doing is they're creating an image in their own mind. And it's that image in their own mind that triggers the emotion, right? So it's the difference between like, and you know this, Teddy, from your experience with, you know, your own personal development journey and whatnot, that like visualization is enormously powerful. It's enormously powerful because it triggers emotion, right? Because it triggers emotion. When you can picture things in your mind's eye, it does a lot of things in a lot of different instances, but one of the things that it does is it conjures the, it triggers, excuse me, emotion. And so what we want to do is we don't want to rely on people doing that. We want to try to paint a picture for them, but we need to be careful that we're not painting a picture that is too narrow or dimensionalizing it where it's too narrow and it doesn't apply. So something like if we said, you know, you'll be getting consistent sales every day, imagine you jump on your MacBook as you sip your morning coffee, you log in, you're Gmail account, and you see dozens of sales which came in while you were sleeping. That's a dimensionalized benefit. That's a, that's dimensionalizing the experience of, of, of waking up every day and seeing sales, but it's not universal enough because the person that says PC disconnect, I don't drink coffee, I drink tea, I don't use Gmail, I use Outlook, right? Like, I'm not trying to generate dozens of sales. I only want a few high ticket sales. And so how do you take this dimensionalized, you know, benefit and how do you keep it universal enough? How do you still dimensionalize it so that somebody could fill in the little details, right? Somebody can fill in the little details, but you're giving them a framework to do that. And so you can take this same thing right here and it just becomes imagine you, you jump on your computer as you sip your favorite morning beverage, you log in to check your sales stats from the night before and you see that you have more sales than you ever thought you would in a single day. And so you jump on your computer, right? To some people that, you know, you might even say, right, you jump on the internet, allowing them to fill in, oh yeah, I do that in the morning on my laptop or I get on my desktop or I get on my Mac book or whatever. As you sip your favorite morning beverage, maybe they have just water in the morning, maybe they have, you know, tea, maybe it is coffee, you log in to check your, your sales stats, they might log into their merchant account, they might log into their CRM, they might log in to click funnels or something like that, right? And then you see more sales than you ever thought you would in a single day, leaving it open enough for the people that like, whether they want to sell a whole bunch, or they want to sell a small number of high ticket people, you're still giving them the ability, like they're still an experience, there's still something happening, it's still, you're dimensionalizing it and the thought of me getting up in the morning and yeah man, jumping on my iPad and sipping my, you know, wacky tea and then going up pulling up my dashboard and seeing stats and knowing that and every day, that's your experience, like wow, that's a picture that gets me excited. And so does that make sense? Yeah. Okay, 100%. Okay. And so that's the, you know, the part that I really wanted to just really drive home for you guys is that it's not just being verb driven, it is starting with what do I want to, you know, what do I want to try to convey and like, what's that image? You know what I mean? Like, you know, and usually with, there's always a, there's always an added, you know, an added bit of like, do I want to convey, yeah, yeah, they're going to experience the result, but do I want to convey that it's easy, that it's rapid, that right, that it's simple, that it's passive, right, because those things, those different things or what I want to convey is going to play a role in which verb I choose, as is also what is commonly said, what's common out there, that I don't want to, I don't want to model because I don't want it to feel the same, I don't want it to have the same vibe. But so underneath everything, there is strategy. Underneath every word, there is strategy, especially verbs. Cool? Awesome. Okay. So with that, I know Matt, you, I don't believe we're on the, we're able to make the copy workshop. So just want to hear from you, Chris and Teddy, what were your, your, your biggest takeaways from the workshop and, and why? So start with you, Chris. For me, it was, it was really the unique mechanism, not in the concept of the unique mechanism, but in the superiority of the unique mechanism and talking about that specifically in the marketing theses. Yeah. That to me was just a game changer because I realized, oh, I've been screwing it up all this time. I'm not trying to sell the product in the marketing theses, and I should be talking about the superiority of the unique mechanism in the marketing theses, because once they believe that, the product is the fulfillment device for the unique mechanism. Yeah. Done. Yeah. I was like, okay, I got a change. So I'm the last couple of days, I've re, I revamped everything, my whole process, he's for doing everything. Okay. That's, I mean, I love hearing that. That's like straight, you know, straight E five, you know, method like, right. And, and I think somebody had said it during the, during the workshop when we were talking about, when we were talking about that, right, that you're ultimately my approach always is always right. And this is in the context of front end, not back end. So we're presenting, you know, a unique mechanism, a different way, a different opportunity, blah, blah, blah, could be tied to a catalyst like we talked about or not. But I'm always my whole job is just to get buy in on the unique mechanism first, like I, my whole thought process is always this, like, I always think about, you've seen that timeline of the, you know, the E five timeline, the 75% and 25% the 25% being the offer. Well, you know, the beauty is, and this is a question that I've gotten, I've gotten asked many times before, they say, like, they'll say, how does the offer, how does the offer, let's say the offer is for a course on the E five method, you know, the masterclass, or let's say the offer is for a discovery call or a strategy call to learn how to use the E five method. And they'll, they'll say, like, the take those two, Todd, and they'll say, what's the difference then in your strategy in that first 75% of the campaign. And the answer is no difference, no difference whatsoever, the offer, what's being offered in the thing, whether it's a strategy call to learn how to implement the E five method in your business, or whether it is to buy a course so that you can learn how to implement the E five method in your business. My job before I make the offer is to get buy in on the E five method is to get buy in on the mechanism and to show them why and how the mechanism will give them the result that they want better, faster, more consistently, more predictably than any other approach. And so right, the beauty is when you do it that way is not only do you get not only do you, you know, you turn their desire for a result into demand for your product because they now want the mechanism, right. And so we haven't been talking about the product at all. We're not discussing the product, you know, talking about how great the product is, we're just getting them to understand the value and power and magnitude of using the the unique mechanism. The other thing and another big part of that is that because you're not talking about a product and trying to convince them of how great the product is, but you're talking about a method, a process, a system, you are, you're not, you're not in pitch mode. So their sales resistance, their reactance is very low to that because you're educating, you're just like, Hey, I've got like, you know, like, whether you present it like, look, I discovered this new method that just killing everything else for, you know, getting big biceps. Let me tell you about this thing. It's called blah, blah, blah. And it's so much better than everything else. And here's why it's so much better. Here's how it works. Here's what you don't need to do. It stimulates this, it does this, it does it, you're educating, right? We know that what we're really doing, we're using education based marketing, it's marketing, it's marketing, because we're educating only on one thing, which is the unique mechanism, everything that we educate them on in some way, shape or form is getting them to see the value benefit payoff and superiority of the mechanism. That's what makes it marketing because we're leading them to our our thesis, but we're using education to do it, right? We're saying like, you know, the beauty, it gets attention. Why does it get attention better than anything else? Because it appeals to this and this and not only that, but it is compliant with the terms of that. Therefore, you never have to worry about getting your account banned or shut off like the typical methods out there. Not only that, it immediately conveys what it is that makes you different. Why? Because it's based on what's called a unique mechanism. That's a different way of presenting your product. And so it gives your prospects hope, unlike the typical approach, which, and so you're educating, you're just really teaching them about the superiority of the mechanism. And then when you've led them to the point where they're like, yeah, okay, I, I, I, or you believe you've led them to the point where they're like, okay, I get it. I see it, man. Sounds sounds like, man, that's, that's the way that I want to go. Now, if you were to, and I've said this many times before, if you were to cut out that last 25%, and you, right, meaning it's there, there would be no offer, you better believe that when done right, people will say, wow, man, thank you so much for like, I get it. I need to go out now and find out how do I, where do I go and learn this E5 method? Or how do I now like, what do I do? I gotta, I gotta implement this. But the feel of a properly done campaign like that is you just shared incredibly valuable, valuable information that opened my eyes to a better way to go about getting what it is that I want. Thank you. That was awesome. Right. And so that's a, that's what happens if you were to take out the offer, but when you take out the offer, now they're grateful that you just gave them that final piece that you made it simple for them to like to, to get it and use it. And so the point is that it's education based marketing, because all we're doing is educating on one thing. And that is the superiority value and effectiveness of that unique mechanism, proving the thesis. And once we feel like we've done that job, boom, we go right into the offer, which is the only place for them to get it. And so beautiful thing. Anything else? Was there, give me one other big takeaway for you besides that? It was basically like going from black and white to color for me. That's really as simple as I can say it. I mean, just in the exercise you went over today, like for me, I'm going to put that in my notes. So every single sentence that I'm constructing, I want to make sure I capture that picture. And then I use the best words that can dial the color of that picture in, if you will. And just systematizing that for myself. So when I write copy, it's just like, like you said, we're the director in our reader's minds, and they see these images and they feel it. So yeah, just like real life to everything. Yeah. Awesome. Awesome. I love it. That's why also as a side note, this is why the, see the verb choice and the difference in verbs and the impact on people of different verbs. This is why I feel that it's going to take a long time for AI to be able to nail all the nuances. While AI, while you can, like today, you can already use technology to have usable copy, like where it's coherent, it's usable copy. I don't think that, I think it's going to be a while before we see the nuance in the, in the word or words being used to bring about like the right impact because there's so much at play in that, in that right choice, that right word choice. But you get it. How about you, Teddy? What was the big, what was one big takeaway for you? So, well, come on, I'm sorry, three, but certainly simplification, right? Like, you know, clear promises. That's still the thing that I've always struggled with in terms of being too verbose or stacking too many things in a sentence and run on sentences and all that kind of stuff. So, that certainly got hammered home. Keeping the mechanism blind or at least making a common mechanism feel unique. You know, having it, you know, and by the way, the simplification, like literally came up, really got hammered home last night on the Q&A, self-defense guy, like, you know, just like him just even struggling to explain it naturally, never mind in the copy. For this late, yeah, yeah, totally get it. And the third biggest thing was the part about incongruency, about, you know, how you're making a claim that, hey, whatever, you know, do this, follow this procedure, you're going to make $10,000 a month, and the guarantee says, and if you don't make $10,000 a month, like that whole incongruency was big. Yeah, that's awesome. I love that. So, going back to the unique mechanism thing though, and superiority, like something came up for me, which was, you know, because we always talk about, well, you know, nobody wants the number the number two or three solution, but that's not always true. Like, you can't always afford the number one solution in a certain case, right? So, when you're not trying to be the premium solution or the number two, you want to be the number two or number three, is there anything different you need to do? So, yeah, it's a great question, man. I think I talked about, maybe I didn't talk about it on the workshop. I can't remember where I talked about it. But so, here's what I would say to that or my perspective on that. And I do think that it's a great question, right? I do think that it's a great question in, like, what if I can't afford, you know, I think I gave the example of, you know, when I had to replace, you know, I moved into the new house and then I had to replace one of the AC units right away, right? And like, you could go to the extreme with an AC unit, I'm sure you know this, Teddy, right? Like, you know, they got AC units that like cost more than my daughter's car, you know what I mean? And I'm like, look, man, unless this thing is going to clean my house and pump out $20 bills every day, right? There's no way that that's happening. Unless you can convince me there's a valuable reason, like, benefit. And so, but what I would say to you is that let's say that I am, let's say there's, you know, the, whatever, the train, I think it's called, TRA, any brand of, you know, like the top of the line thing. And we're selling, you know, we're selling AC units in the five to $10,000 range. Well, I still believe, Teddy, that if I'm looking for, okay, so I'm looking for an AC unit, call it five to 10,000, you know, eight grand, let's call it up to 10 grand, I'm willing to spend on an AC, right? And so I would still say that I'm still looking for the best in that range. Right. You know what I mean? So what I'm not looking for is I'm not like, if there are three tiers to AC units, right? The best is this, this third, you know, the tier three, that's the best of the best. I might feel like, you know, that's not what I need. So it's almost very similar to, you know, in my mind, I go to, you did a great job with the, remember when you gave me the three tiers of tracking solutions, right? And you were like, all right, for somebody just starting out, they're spending this much money on traffic, like they go with this one. But once they're at, you know, 10 grand a month, they want to go here. And then the mother load is like what you did, what you did for us, right? And so there's a lot of people that are like, you know, I want the best that is appropriate for me, right? I want the best that's appropriate for me. So that's out of like, that's not what I need, you know, and that's the way I felt with certain AC units. I'm like, you know, I don't, you know, like, you know, I don't need whatever, whatever, I don't need the thing that's buried in my backyard and blah, blah, blah. And so I was like, this is what I need. This is what I'm looking for. This is the range I want to spend up to 10 grand, right? What's the best based on that? That's so I still believe that people are looking for the the best within what it is that they want. I don't know if that answers the question or if that I was having a philosophical conversation with Sally Hogs had about it last last week, because, you know, she's coming out with her new book. It's different is better than better. And I'm like, well, yeah, but you need different and better. She's like, Oh, I might challenge you on that. Like, sometimes just being different enough is is enough. Well, I would say this that, right, and she's, you know, brilliant and, and, and, and far smarter than I am in terms of that area of getting attention and being, you know, fascinating to your audience and all that stuff. But what I would say is that different is certainly better than better when it comes to getting attention, but not when it comes to producing conversion. Like, because different is not enough to me just because it's different, just because there's a different dashboard, like, or a different user interface, or it's built on a different, you know, platform, or what I care about, like, because different that here's the thing, right, if you really want to boil it down, different is an element or has to do with the product. It's different. But what does that mean to me? Like, why do I care about that difference? And so, right, when there is a, when the difference makes it superior, which is what we look for with unique mechanism, right, like the unique mechanism, like, look, you could say that different is not always superior, but superior is always different. If it, right, superior has to be different. If it's superior, like, you can't have superior in the same, right, right? You know what I mean? But to me, like, I think, yeah, different definitely gets attention without without question. But then what prospects want to see is, okay, well, now, like, how valuable is this difference? Like, meaning, what does it mean to me? And so, I think that you do need both. And that we talk about that in E five, that, you know, the, the different this is different is what gets attention. It's what stops people long enough to say, let me see what this is about. And now what they're ultimately looking for is I want to know if this is better than what it is that I'm doing or better than what it is that I'm considering. And better, right, has different nuances, like, I might be looking for better in terms of speed, better in terms of ease, better in terms of cost. But I'm looking different, right. And the reason why I believe that different gets attention is because there is a hope that maybe there will be something better in that different thing compared to what it is that I'm using or considering in some area that'll make me want to switch to that. It's like kind of like, right. And I'm the, you know, you know, I'm the the software, the apps, that's, that's my nemesis. I'm always like, I'm always attracted to these new apps. And when I see things like different, because I'm always like, I wonder if this thing does X, or I wonder if this thing will solve Y, right. And so I'm still looking, it's different. And so, oh, maybe it's different, maybe then it will allow me to do element OP. And so to me, when you see different, and then like, and I've seen this before with apps, like, like tons, right, where it's, it's positioned as different. And then I'm like, all right, it's got the same feature set as sales made. And so it's not, you know, it looks different, but that difference is insignificant to me. Like, because I'm not, I'm not trying to replace it for a different look. I'm trying to replace it for a different benefit. But she's certainly brilliant in that. And when it comes to getting attention and conveying difference, and she's amazing at that. And so, yeah, incredible. And so, all right, that's it. You guys are out here.
